humanities_role_explanation: Uses factual QA items from humanities disciplines (such as history, philosophy, music, architecture, and world religions) to evaluate the general long-form factual accuracy of large language models.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: A unimodal text-based benchmark consisting of fact-seeking prompts designed to elicit multi-paragraph, long-form text responses.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Decomposes long-form generative responses into independent, self-contained atomic facts, filters them for prompt relevance, and verifies each claim against Google Search using an iterative, multi-step LLM-agent reasoning process.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates long-form factuality across 38 diverse topics spanning STEM, social sciences, humanities, and other general-knowledge domains.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates thirteen generative models across four major families: Gemini (Ultra, Pro), GPT (4-Turbo, 4, 3.5), Claude (3-Opus, 3-Sonnet, 3-Haiku, 2.1, 2.0, Instant), and PaLM-2.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The LLMs themselves are the primary target systems being benchmarked for long-form generation factuality.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes multiple ASJC areas: History (20th-century events, general history), Philosophy (philosophy, moral disputes, business ethics), Religious Studies (world religions), and Visual Arts and Performing Arts (movies, music, architecture).

quantitative_metrics.note: Calculates precision (percentage of supported facts), recall (measured relative to a target length parameter K), the unified F1@K score, and human-agreement percentages.

published_after_march_2023.note: Preprint first published in March 2024 and presented at NeurIPS 2024.

english_language.note: Both the research paper and the generated LongFact prompt set are in English.

summary: This paper presents LongFact, a new prompt set spanning 38 topics designed to test the factuality of large language models in generating long-form answers. To automate evaluation, the authors introduce SAFE (Search-Augmented Factuality Evaluator), which uses an LLM to split answers into atomic facts and verify them using Google Search, as well as an F1@K metric to balance factual precision with a user-preferred response length.
key_findings: The automated evaluator SAFE matches or beats human crowd-workers, agreeing with human annotations 72% of the time and winning 76% of disagreement cases, while being more than 20 times cheaper ($0.19 vs $4.00 per response). Benchmarking 13 models reveals that larger models (such as GPT-4-Turbo, Gemini-Ultra, and PaLM-2-L-IT-RLHF) perform best, showing that scaling primarily improves factual recall.
